original_abstract: The following critical questions are posed: is hope the antidote of dread and despair or a kind of escapism from the harsh realities of anguish and suffering? What is meant by hope in Christian spirituality and how is hope connected to a theology of the resurrection? Is resurrection hope merely a kind of cheap triumphantalism and variant of a theologia gloriae? The basic assumption is that the notion of the resurrection can contribute to ‘the thickening of alternative stories of faith’. A theologia resurrectionis is about the reframing of life by means of a radical paradox: ‘Where, O death is your victory? Where, O death is your sting?’ If pastoral caregiving is indeed about change and hope, the resurrection describes an ontology of hope by which human beings are transformed into a total new being. Beyond the discriminating and stigmatising categories of many social and cultural discourses on our being human, resurrection theology defines hope as a new state of mind and being. The identity of human beings is therefore not determined by descent, gender, race or social status, but by eschatology (new creation.) Hope care is primarily about a new courage to be. It opens up different frameworks for meaningful living within the realm of human suffering.
humanized_abstract: Hope and Resurrection Hope and dread/dispair: Is hope an antidote to all forms of human dread and despair, or is it a form of escapism? How do we relate to “hope” in Christian spirituality, and what is the relationship between “hope” and a resurrection theology? Is resurrection hope cheap triumphalism and a theologia gloriae variant? Our presupposition here is that the resurrection can be part of “thickening alternative faith stories”. A theologia resurrectionis is about reframing life through a radical paradox that Paul recalls from a hymn “Where O death is your victory? Where O death is your sting?” We propose that if pastoral caregiving in the context of the world of today is about change and hope; that the resurrection of Jesus Christ gives an ontology of hope that is capable of transforming us into a new human being. Beyond all the labelling and stigmatising discourses that describe our humanity today; resurrection theology affirms hope in the sense of a new way of being. It means that humanity’s identity is not in our DNA; nor in our gender; nor in our race; nor in our social status. We are identified as human beings by our eschatology – the promise of new creation. Hope care has to do with a new kind of courage to be. A new way of orienting ourselves in a world filled with suffering and different forms of alienation. It is about trying to find alternative frameworks and world views in which to live our human lives in the midst of suffering and all forms of alienation.
